title: Deconstructing Precocious Puberty — A Resident Training Module
subtitle: B5 + menarche: the missing variables, targeted workup, MRI rules, and the bone-age treatment decision
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.age: Not documented — the critical missing variable
patient.chiefComplaint: Menarche and Tanner B5 breast development — query precocious puberty
pmh: Tanner staging B5 (adult breast), P3 (pubic hair stage 3)

caseDescription: A girl is referred with menarche (2 June, lasting 2 days) and Tanner B5 (adult) breast development with P3 pubic hair, seen on 27/11/2017. A first-principles ‘glassbox’ teaching approach starts not with the labs but with the missing foundational variables: her chronological age and her height velocity. B5 with menarche is the END-stage of puberty, not its onset — and the interpretation flips entirely depending on age (severe and rapid at 7; early-normal but exceptionally fast at 9.5). The resident’s initial workup (bone-age X-ray; basal LH, FSH, estradiol; pelvic ultrasound; TSH/fT4; 17-OHP) is sound and is refined with a GnRH stimulation test and a targeted DHEAS/testosterone rather than a blanket steroid panel. Imaging and treatment then follow logically: a brain MRI only if central precocious puberty is proven, and GnRH-analog therapy only where the bone age shows growth potential still worth salvaging.
keyFindings: Tanner B5 (adult breast) + menarche + P3 pubic hair = end-stage of puberty, not onset
Chronological age and height velocity NOT documented — the critical missing variables
Initial workup: bone-age X-ray, basal LH/FSH/estradiol, pelvic USG, TSH/fT4, 17-OHP
Refinements needed: GnRH stimulation test; targeted DHEAS + total testosterone
No virilization (no clitoromegaly/severe acne); breast (estrogenic) >> pubic-hair (androgenic) stage
finalDiagnosis: Late-stage, rapidly progressive precocious puberty (B5/menarche) — central vs peripheral to be confirmed by basal/stimulated LH; management hinges on bone age
learningObjectives: Establish chronological age and height velocity before interpreting precocious-puberty labs
Differentiate central from peripheral precocious puberty and target the workup (GnRH stimulation test, DHEAS/testosterone)
Apply brain-MRI indications and use bone age to judge whether GnRH-analog therapy can still salvage final height

items.title: Why the first questions are chronological age and height velocity, not the labs
items.content: Tanner B5 with menarche is the end-stage of pubertal development, not the beginning — so before touching the lab values, anchor the case to two missing foundational variables: the chronological age (CA) and the height velocity. The same B5/menarche picture means very different things by age: at 7 it is rapid and severe; at 9.5 it is early-normal puberty progressing exceptionally fast. Without CA and height velocity you cannot judge severity, predict adult height, or decide on treatment. This is the glassbox discipline: define the foundational inputs before interpreting the outputs.
keyPoints: B5 + menarche = end-stage, not onset.
Always pin chronological age and height velocity first.
Severity and management flip with age (7 vs 9.5).

items.title: Validating the initial workup: why each test was ordered
items.content: The resident’s orders were a strong start. Bone-age X-ray (wrist/hand): the ultimate physiological branch point — a bone age more than 2 SD above chronological age proves the puberty is biologically significant and is accelerating epiphyseal fusion. Basal LH, FSH, estradiol: separate central (LH-dominant) from peripheral (estradiol-dominant with suppressed LH/FSH) puberty. Pelvic ultrasound (uterus/ovaries): a uterine length >3.4 cm or loss of the tubular configuration indicates significant estrogen exposure, and it screens for ovarian cysts or tumors. TSH/fT4: severe primary hypothyroidism can paradoxically cause early menses and breast development (Van Wyk–Grumbach syndrome), as very high TSH cross-reacts with FSH receptors. 17-OHP: screens for congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH).
keyPoints: Bone age >2 SD above CA = biologically significant, accelerating fusion.
Basal LH/FSH/estradiol → central vs peripheral; pelvic USG → estrogen exposure, cysts/tumors.
TSH/fT4 → Van Wyk–Grumbach; 17-OHP → CAH.

items.title: How to refine the workup: the GnRH stimulation test and a targeted steroid panel
items.content: Two refinements. (1) GnRH (agonist) stimulation test: basal LH is often still prepubertal (<0.3 IU/L) even in early true central precocious puberty (CPP), so a stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L is the gold standard to prove central axis activation. (2) Steroid testing: a blanket steroid panel is low-yield here, because the girl is markedly estrogenic (B5) with only mild-to-moderate androgenic features (P3). Order a targeted DHEAS and total testosterone, which is enough to exclude an adrenal or ovarian androgen-secreting tumor. Reserve a complex LC-MS/MS steroid profile for a borderline 17-OHP or rapid virilization (clitoromegaly, severe acne) — both absent here.
keyPoints: Basal LH may be prepubertal; stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L confirms CPP.
Skip the blanket steroid panel; order targeted DHEAS + total testosterone.
Escalate to LC-MS/MS only if 17-OHP borderline or rapid virilization.

items.title: How the physical exam hunts for the etiology
items.content: Look beyond staging and the pelvis. Anthropometry & mapping: plot current height and weight, calculate the mid-parental (target) height, and measure the current height velocity (cm/year). Neurological exam: visual field testing and fundoscopy (optic pathway gliomas), and check for focal deficits or signs of raised intracranial pressure. Dermatological exam (key for peripheral causes): large, irregular ‘coast of Maine’ café-au-lait macules suggest McCune–Albright syndrome (autonomous ovarian cysts); neurofibromas or axillary freckling suggest Neurofibromatosis type 1 (associated with central precocious puberty). Abdomen: palpate for ovarian or adrenal masses.
keyPoints: Plot height/weight, mid-parental height, and height velocity.
Neuro: visual fields + fundoscopy (optic glioma), signs of raised ICP.
Skin: ‘coast of Maine’ café-au-lait → McCune–Albright; NF1 stigmata; palpate for masses.

items.title: How to decide when to image the brain (MRI)
items.content: A brain and pituitary MRI (with contrast) is indicated only if the labs prove central precocious puberty (elevated basal or stimulated LH). Rule of thumb for girls with confirmed CPP: under 6 years — absolute indication (high risk of CNS pathology such as hamartomas and astrocytomas); 6–8 years — debated, but strongly recommended in an aggressive presentation like this one (reaching B5 and menarche this early implies a rapidly progressive variant); over 8 years — usually not indicated unless there are overt neurological symptoms. If instead the biochemistry indicates peripheral precocious puberty (high estradiol with suppressed LH/FSH), do NOT scan the brain — direct imaging to the abdomen and pelvis.
keyPoints: MRI brain only after CPP is biochemically confirmed.
<6 yr absolute; 6–8 yr strongly recommended if aggressive; >8 yr only if neuro symptoms.
Peripheral picture → image abdomen/pelvis, not the brain.

items.title: Why management hinges on bone age: the B5/menarche paradox
items.content: Treatment is a calculation of physiological return on investment. Because this girl has already reached B5 and had menarche, her pubertal development is essentially complete. When NOT to treat: if the bone age is already >12–12.5 years (typical once menarche has occurred), the epiphyses are fusing and a GnRH analog (e.g. leuprolide) yields zero net gain in final adult height; also in slow progressors whose predicted adult height is normal for the family. The resident pitfall is treating purely to stop periods when bone age >12.5 — this imposes menopausal symptoms, reduces peak bone-mass accrual, and does not fix the height deficit. When TO treat: bone age significantly advanced but still <12 years (growth still salvageable); severe psychosocial distress in a very young child (treat to halt menses, counselling that height benefit may be minimal); or rapid progression with predicted adult height actively dropping across centiles.
keyPoints: Bone age >12–12.5 with menarche → GnRH analog gives no height gain; generally don’t treat.
Pitfall: treating only to stop periods causes menopausal symptoms and harms bone mass.
Treat if BA advanced but <12, severe psychosocial distress (very young), or PAH dropping fast.

items.title: The bottom line for the resident
items.content: Do not look at the B5 and panic. Look at the bone-age X-ray. If the skeleton is already about 13 years old, the window to save height is closed and a GnRH analog will not help. You must still diagnose the ‘why’ — exclude a tumor and identify central versus peripheral cause — but the medical intervention for the puberty itself may be entirely conservative at this late stage. Diagnose thoroughly; treat selectively.
keyPoints: The bone age, not the Tanner stage, drives the height decision.
Skeleton already ~13 → height window closed.
Always find the cause; intervene on the puberty only when it changes the outcome.

items.question: Why must chronological age and height velocity be established before interpreting the labs in this girl with B5 and menarche?
items.answer: Because the same end-stage picture (B5 with menarche) carries completely different meaning and management depending on age and tempo. At 7 it is severe, rapid precocious puberty; at 9.5 it is early-normal puberty that is simply progressing fast. Chronological age sets the reference against which bone age and adult-height prediction are judged, and height velocity reveals how fast the process is accelerating. Without these two anchors, the LH, estradiol, and bone-age numbers cannot be interpreted, the severity cannot be graded, and the treat-or-not decision cannot be made.
keyPoints: B5/menarche means different things at 7 vs 9.5.
Chronological age is the reference for bone age and predicted adult height.
Height velocity shows the tempo and the threat to final height.

items.question: How does the GnRH stimulation test establish central precocious puberty?
items.answer: In early true central precocious puberty the basal LH can still look prepubertal (often <0.3 IU/L), so a single random LH may miss an activated axis. The GnRH (or GnRH-agonist) stimulation test challenges the pituitary: if the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis has been switched on centrally, the stimulated LH rises to a pubertal peak — a peak LH >5 IU/L is the gold standard confirming central activation. A blunted, FSH-predominant response instead points away from central precocious puberty. This is what distinguishes true CPP from peripheral causes when the basal labs are equivocal.
keyPoints: Basal LH may be prepubertal even in early CPP.
Stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L confirms central axis activation.
A blunted/FSH-dominant response argues against CPP.

items.question: Why is a brain MRI inappropriate when the biochemistry indicates peripheral precocious puberty?
items.answer: Peripheral precocious puberty is driven by gonadal or adrenal sex-steroid production independent of the central axis — the hallmark is a high estradiol with suppressed LH and FSH. The pathology therefore lies below the brain: in the ovary (cysts, McCune–Albright, tumors) or adrenal. A brain MRI looks for central lesions (hamartomas, gliomas) that are not the cause here, so it adds cost, contrast exposure, and sedation risk for no yield. Imaging should instead be directed at the abdomen and pelvis, where the responsible lesion actually is.
keyPoints: Peripheral PP = high estradiol with suppressed LH/FSH (axis not activated).
The lesion is gonadal/adrenal, not central — a brain MRI is low-yield.
Image the abdomen/pelvis instead.

items.question: How does the bone age determine whether GnRH-analog therapy can still salvage final height?
items.answer: GnRH analogs help final height only by pausing puberty while growth plates are still open, buying time for more linear growth before fusion. The bone age estimates how much epiphyseal growth potential remains. If the bone age is significantly advanced but still under ~12 years, plates are open and there is height to salvage, so treatment can help. Once menarche has occurred and bone age is ~12.5 years or more, the epiphyses are fusing or fused — suppressing puberty now yields essentially zero net height gain, so a GnRH analog is not justified for height. The decision is read off the bone-age film, not the Tanner stage.
keyPoints: GnRH analogs add height only while growth plates remain open.
BA advanced but <12 → still salvageable; treat.
BA ≥12.5 with menarche → plates fusing, no net height gain; don’t treat for height.

items.question: Which targeted tests exclude an androgen-secreting tumor here, and when would you escalate to a full steroid profile?
items.answer: Order DHEAS and total testosterone — together these are sufficient to screen for an adrenal or ovarian androgen-secreting tumor, and they fit this estrogen-dominant (B5) girl with only mild androgenic features (P3). Escalate to a complex LC-MS/MS steroid profile only if the 17-OHP is borderline (to pursue CAH variants) or if there is rapid virilization such as clitoromegaly or severe acne — neither of which is present in this case. A blanket steroid panel up front is low-yield.

items.stem: A girl presents with Tanner B5 breast development and menarche. What does this staging indicate about her pubertal timeline?
options: She is at the very onset of puberty
She is at the end-stage of pubertal development, which is essentially complete
She has not yet entered puberty
Staging cannot indicate timeline
items.explanation: Tanner B5 with menarche represents the end-stage of puberty, not its beginning — a key reframe, because it means most development (and growth) has already occurred, which heavily shapes the height-salvage and treatment decision.

items.stem: What is the gold-standard test to prove central activation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis when the basal LH is prepubertal?
options: A single random basal LH
A GnRH (agonist) stimulation test showing a peak LH >5 IU/L
Pelvic ultrasound
Serum DHEAS
items.explanation: Basal LH is often still prepubertal (<0.3 IU/L) in early central precocious puberty, so a GnRH stimulation test is used: a stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L confirms central axis activation. Ultrasound and DHEAS address other questions.

items.stem: Which condition can paradoxically cause early breast development and menses through TSH cross-reacting with FSH receptors?
options: Severe primary hypothyroidism (Van Wyk–Grumbach syndrome)
Hyperthyroidism
Cushing syndrome
Type 1 diabetes
items.explanation: In severe primary hypothyroidism, very high TSH can cross-react with FSH receptors to cause precocious breast development and menses — Van Wyk–Grumbach syndrome — which is why TSH/fT4 are part of the workup.

items.stem: Large, irregular ‘coast of Maine’ café-au-lait macules in a girl with precocious puberty should raise suspicion of which diagnosis?
options: Neurofibromatosis type 1
McCune–Albright syndrome (autonomous ovarian cysts)
Tuberous sclerosis
Addison disease
items.explanation: ‘Coast of Maine’ (large, irregular-bordered) café-au-lait macules suggest McCune–Albright syndrome, a peripheral cause from autonomous ovarian cysts. Smooth ‘coast of California’ macules with axillary freckling/neurofibromas instead suggest NF1 (a central association).

items.stem: In a girl with biochemically confirmed central precocious puberty, at what age is brain MRI an absolute indication?
options: Under 6 years
Any age above 10
Only after menarche
It is never indicated
items.explanation: Under 6 years, brain MRI is an absolute indication in confirmed CPP because of the high risk of CNS pathology (hamartomas, astrocytomas). From 6–8 years it is debated but strongly recommended in aggressive presentations; over 8 years it is usually reserved for those with neurological symptoms.

items.stem: Her bone age is ~12.5 years and she has already had menarche. What is the most appropriate stance on GnRH-analog therapy for height?
options: Start a GnRH analog — it will substantially increase final height
Do not use a GnRH analog for height; at this fused/fusing stage it yields no net height gain — still diagnose the cause and exclude a tumor
Double the GnRH-analog dose to overcome the advanced bone age
Treat indefinitely purely to stop the periods
items.explanation: Once bone age is ~12.5 years with menarche, the epiphyses are fusing, so a GnRH analog gives essentially zero net height gain. Management of the puberty itself is conservative; the priority is to diagnose the cause and exclude a tumor. Treating just to stop periods imposes menopausal symptoms and harms bone mass without fixing height.

items.stem: Given B5 breast development far exceeding P3 pubic hair, with no virilization, which targeted labs best exclude an androgen-secreting tumor?
options: A blanket multi-analyte steroid panel up front
DHEAS and total testosterone
Repeat estradiol only
24-hour urinary free cortisol
items.explanation: A targeted DHEAS and total testosterone are sufficient to screen for an adrenal/ovarian androgen-secreting tumor in this estrogen-dominant girl without virilization. A blanket steroid panel is low-yield, and a full LC-MS/MS profile is reserved for borderline 17-OHP or rapid virilization.

items.stem: Which biochemical pattern indicates PERIPHERAL precocious puberty?
options: High estradiol with suppressed LH and FSH
High LH with a brisk GnRH-stimulated response
Low estradiol with high LH and FSH
Normal estradiol with normal gonadotropins
items.explanation: Peripheral precocious puberty is driven by autonomous gonadal/adrenal sex-steroid production, so estradiol is high while LH and FSH are suppressed. A high or briskly stimulated LH instead indicates central precocious puberty.

items.statement: A GnRH-stimulated peak LH greater than 5 IU/L supports a diagnosis of central precocious puberty.
items.explanation: True. Because basal LH may be prepubertal in early CPP, a stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L is the gold-standard evidence of central axis activation.

items.statement: Every girl with precocious puberty should undergo a brain MRI regardless of whether the cause is central or peripheral.
items.explanation: False. Brain MRI is indicated only once central precocious puberty is biochemically confirmed; for a peripheral picture (high estradiol, suppressed LH/FSH), imaging should target the abdomen/pelvis instead.

items.statement: Once bone age exceeds about 12.5 years and menarche has occurred, a GnRH analog still meaningfully increases final adult height.
items.explanation: False. At this fused/fusing epiphyseal stage there is no growth potential left to salvage, so a GnRH analog yields essentially zero net height gain.

items.statement: Severe primary hypothyroidism can cause precocious breast development and menses (Van Wyk–Grumbach syndrome).
items.explanation: True. Markedly elevated TSH can cross-react with FSH receptors, producing precocious breast development and menses — reversible with thyroid hormone replacement.

items.statement: A blanket multi-analyte steroid panel is the best first-line test in this estrogen-dominant girl with no virilization.
items.explanation: False. A blanket panel is low-yield here; targeted DHEAS and total testosterone are appropriate, with a full LC-MS/MS profile reserved for borderline 17-OHP or rapid virilization.

items.statement: Calculating height velocity and mid-parental (target) height is part of the precocious-puberty assessment.
items.explanation: True. Plotting growth, computing the mid-parental height, and measuring height velocity are essential to grade severity and predict the threat to final adult height.

items.assertion: A GnRH analog should generally not be started for height in this patient if her bone age is already about 12.5 years with menarche achieved.
items.reason: At a fused or fusing epiphyseal stage, GnRH-analog therapy yields no net gain in final adult height.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Since GnRH analogs improve height only while growth plates remain open, an advanced bone age with menarche means there is no height to salvage — so the therapy is not justified for that purpose, even though the cause must still be diagnosed.

items.stem: Why must chronological age and height velocity be established before interpreting laboratory results in a girl with B5 and menarche?
options: A) To determine the reference for bone age and adult height prediction and assess the tempo of puberty progression
B) To diagnose the underlying cause of precocious puberty
C) To measure the levels of LH and estradiol accurately
D) To predict the timing of future menarche
items.explanation: Chronological age provides the reference for interpreting bone age and predicting adult height, while height velocity indicates the tempo of puberty progression, both critical for grading severity and treatment decisions.

items.stem: What is the gold standard criterion for confirming central activation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis in the GnRH stimulation test for central precocious puberty?
options: A. Basal LH level >0.3 IU/L
B. Stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L
C. Stimulated FSH predominance over LH
D. Basal FSH level >10 IU/L
items.explanation: A stimulated peak LH >5 IU/L confirms central axis activation, as it indicates hypothalamic stimulation. Basal LH may be prepubertal in early CPP. FSH predominance suggests peripheral causes, and basal FSH is not a diagnostic criterion.

items.stem: Why is a brain MRI not indicated in a patient with peripheral precocious puberty and suppressed LH/FSH levels?
options: A) To rule out central nervous system tumors
B) To evaluate for hypothalamic dysfunction
C) Because the pathology is gonadal or adrenal, not central
D) To assess for pituitary gland abnormalities
items.explanation: Peripheral precocious puberty is caused by gonadal or adrenal sex-steroid production (e.g., ovarian cysts, adrenal tumors) with suppressed LH/FSH, not central axis activation. Brain MRI is unnecessary as the lesion is not in the CNS; imaging should focus on abdomen/pelvis.

items.stem: At what bone age threshold is GnRH-analog therapy most likely to salvage final height in a prepubertal child?
options: A) Bone age >12.5 years with menarche
B) Bone age matching chronological age
C) Bone age <12 years with open growth plates
D) Bone age >14 years with advanced skeletal maturity
items.explanation: GnRH analogs add height only while growth plates remain open. Bone age <12 years indicates significant remaining growth potential, whereas bone age ≥12.5 years with menarche signifies epiphyseal fusion or imminent fusion, making therapy ineffective for height salvage.

items.stem: Which tests are most appropriate to exclude an androgen-secreting tumor in this estrogen-dominant girl with mild androgenic features, and under what circumstances should a full steroid profile be ordered?
options: A) 17-OHP and cortisol; escalate if clitoromegaly is present
B) DHEAS and free testosterone; escalate if hirsutism worsens over months
C) DHEAS and total testosterone; escalate if 17-OHP is borderline or virilization is rapid
D) 17-OHP and testosterone; escalate only if CAH is suspected
items.explanation: DHEAS and total testosterone are sufficient for initial screening of adrenal/ovarian androgen-secreting tumors. Escalation to LC-MS/MS steroid profiling is indicated only when 17-OHP is borderline (suggesting CAH variants) or there is rapid virilization (e.g., clitoromegaly), which are not present here. Options A and D use inappropriate initial tests, while B incorrectly delays escalation.
